Use caching to reduce database load.
Design systems assuming failures will happen.
Prefer eventual consistency when strong consistency isn't required.
Use circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.
Start with a monolith, then extract microservices as needed.
Always design APIs with versioning from the start.
Use message queues to decouple services and absorb traffic spikes.
Implement health checks for all your services.
Use consistent hashing for distributed cache partitioning.
Database indexes speed up reads but slow down writes — balance accordingly.
Use CDNs to serve static content closer to users.
Design your data model around your access patterns, not the other way around.
Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures.
Use write-ahead logs for crash recovery in databases.
Shard your database horizontally when vertical scaling hits its limits.
Separate read and write workloads using CQRS when necessary.
Use heartbeats and gossip protocols for failure detection in distributed systems.
Design idempotent APIs so retries don't cause duplicate operations.
Apply the principle of least privilege to all service-to-service communication.
Monitor the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation.
Use feature flags to decouple deployments from releases.
Prefer asynchronous communication between services when possible.
Use connection pooling to avoid exhausting database connections.
Design for observability: logs, metrics, and traces from day one.
Apply backpressure to protect downstream services from overload.
